1: The oracle addresses all Jews throughout Egypt — four geographic areas: Migdol (northeast delta), Tahpanhes (northern delta, where the remnant fled), Noph (Memphis, central Egypt), and Pathros (paṭrôs, Upper Egypt — the south). The comprehensive geographic sweep indicates a substantial Jewish diaspora already in Egypt before the post-fall remnant arrived. The oracle is pan-Egyptian, not limited to the Tahpanhes colony.
2: ʾattem rĕʾîtem ʾēt kol-hārāʿâ ʾăšer hēbēʾtî ʿal-Yĕrûšālaim — "You have seen all the disaster I brought on Jerusalem and all the cities of Judah." The oracle begins with the witnessed evidence: they saw Jerusalem destroyed. Wĕhinnām ḥorbâ hayyôm hazzeh wĕʾên yôšēb bāhem — "and they lie desolate this day, with no one living in them." The desolation is presented as visible, present, and irrefutable.
3: mippĕnê rāʿātām ʾăšer ʿāśû lĕhaʿăsîsēnî lāleket lĕqaṭṭēr laʿăbōd lēʾlōhîm ʾăḥērîm — "because of the wickedness they committed, provoking me to anger by going to burn offerings to other gods — gods that neither they nor you nor your ancestors had known." The repetition "neither they nor you nor your ancestors" (hēmmâ wĕʾattem wĕʾăbôtêkem) is comprehensive across time. The gods are defined by their unknownness: they are not the God of the covenant, not the God of Israel's history.
4: wĕʾešlaḥ ʾălêkem ʾet-kol-ʿăbādây hannĕbîʾîm haškēm wĕšālôaḥ — "Yet I sent you all my servants the prophets, again and again, urgently (haškēm wĕšālôaḥ)." The infinitive absolute construction (haškēm wĕšālôaḥ, rising early and sending) conveys persistent, urgent repetition — the standard Jeremianic formula for YHWH's comprehensive prophetic effort. ʾAl-tāʿăśû ʾet-haddebet hammĕtôrāʿâ hazzōʾt ʾăšer śānēʾtî — "Do not do this detestable thing that I hate" — YHWH's stated emotional response to the idolatry: hatred of the detestable.
5: wĕlōʾ šāmĕʿû wĕlōʾ hiṭṭû ʾet-ʾoznām lāšûb mērāʿātām — "But they did not listen or pay attention, to turn from their wickedness and stop making offerings to other gods." The refrain of non-listening (lōʾ šāmĕʿû wĕlōʾ hiṭṭû ʾet-ʾoznām) runs throughout Jeremiah (7:24, 11:8, 17:23, 25:4). The covenant hearing-obedience formula negated: they had ears but did not incline them. The MKT preserves "did not listen or pay attention" as two distinct acts of covenant failure.
6: wayyittāk ʾappî ûḥămātî wattibʿar bĕʿārê Yĕhûdâ — "Therefore my wrath and my anger were poured out and ignited in the cities of Judah." The spatial progression — cities of Judah → streets of Jerusalem — traces the covenant judgment from the periphery to the center. The result: "they became the desolation and waste that they are today" (lĕḥorbâ ûlišmāmâ kayyôm hazzeh). The present-tense desolation is the evidence for the past judgment and the warning about the future.
7: wĕʿattâ kōh-ʾāmar YHWH ʾĕlōhê ṣĕbāʾôt ʾĕlōhê Yiśrāʾēl — the transition to direct address. Lāmâ ʾattem ʿōśîm rāʿâ gĕdôlâ ʾel-napšôtêkem — "Why are you doing this great harm to yourselves?" The harm of idolatry is self-harm (ʾel-napšôtêkem, to your own souls). The vocabulary of covenant suicide: cutting off man and woman, child and infant from Judah — leaving no remnant (lĕbiltî hišʾîr lākem šĕʾērît).
8: lĕhaʿăsîsēnî bĕmaʿăśê yĕdêkem lĕqaṭṭēr lēʾlōhîm ʾăḥērîm bĕʾerets Miṣrayim — "You provoke me to anger with your own handiwork by burning offerings to other gods in the land of Egypt." Lĕmaʿan hikrît lākem ûlĕmaʿan hĕyôtkem lĕqĕlālâ ûlĕḥerpâ bĕkōl gôyê hāʾārets — the terrible irony: the intent of their idol-worship was security and blessing; the result is to make themselves a curse and reproach among all nations.
9: hăšĕkaḥtem ʾet-rāʿôt ʾăbôtêkem — "Have you forgotten the wickedness of your ancestors?" The memorial function of covenant history: the past wickedness and its consequences should be remembered as warning. The list: ancestors, kings of Judah, their wives, your own wickedness, the wickedness of your wives — comprehensive across time and gender. The MKT preserves the parallel structure of the comprehensive list.
10: lōʾ dukkaʾû ʿad hayyôm hazzeh — "They have not been humbled even to this day." Dākaʾ (be crushed, humbled, broken) — not broken by the disasters they have witnessed. Wĕlōʾ yārĕʾû wĕlōʾ hālĕkû bĕtôrātî ûbĕḥuqqôtay ʾăšer nātattî lĕpânêkem ûlĕpĕnê ʾăbôtêkem — the three covenant-failure verbs: did not fear, did not walk in the Torah, did not keep the statutes. The MKT renders the full triad.
11: hinnĕnî śōmēm ʾet-pānay bākem lĕrāʿâ — "I am going to set my face against you for disaster" — YHWH's face turned in judgment rather than blessing. Ûlĕhakrît ʾet-kol-Yĕhûdâ (to cut off all Judah) — the remnant-cutting-off oracle. The remnant in Egypt is not the seed of restoration; it is the object of the final judgment on the covenant community that chose Egypt.
12: wĕlāqaḥtî ʾet-šĕʾērît Yĕhûdâ ʾăšer śāmû pĕnêhem lāboʾ ʾerets Miṣrayim — "I will take the remnant of Judah who resolved to go to Egypt to live there, and they shall all be consumed." The twofold fate: baḥereb ûbārāʿāb yittammû (by sword and famine they shall be finished). The series of shame-vocabulary: lĕqĕlālâ lĕʾālâ lĕḥerpâ lĕḥerpâ — curse, waste, horror, reproach. Each noun represents a social dimension of the covenantal humiliation.
13: The parallel with Jerusalem is explicit: kĕʾăšer pāqadtî ʿal-Yĕrûšālaim (as I punished Jerusalem). The Egypt exiles will experience exactly what they watched happen to Jerusalem. The three agents — sword, famine, plague — recur for the last time in Jeremiah's Egyptian ministry as the covenant-curse triad.
14: wĕlōʾ yihyeh pālîṭ ûśārîd lišʾērît Yĕhûdâ habbāʾîm lāgûr šām bĕʾerets Miṣrayim — "None of the remnant of Judah who have come to Egypt to live there will escape or survive to return to the land of Judah"; the word pālîṭ (escapee) and śārîd (survivor) — the vocabulary of those who narrowly survived disaster. "Which they long to return to and live in" — the tragic irony: they long for the land but will die without reaching it, because they fled the land.
15: The community's counter-response: the men who knew their wives had been making offerings to the queen of heaven, "and all the women standing there — a large assembly" (qāhāl gādôl). The formal assembly that petitioned YHWH in 42:1 is now the assembly that formally rejects the oracle. The MKT preserves "large assembly" to maintain the deliberate contrast with the assembly that seemed to commit to obedience.
16: haddābār ʾăšer dibbartā ʾēlênû bĕšēm YHWH ʾênennû šōmĕʿîm ʾēlêkā — "As for the word you have spoken to us in the name of the LORD, we will not listen to you." The formal rejection of the prophetic word using the standard oracle-reception formula but negated: "in the name of the LORD" (bĕšēm YHWH) is acknowledged, then rejected. The rejection is not of Jeremiah's personal opinion but of YHWH's named word.
17: kî ʿāśôh naʿăśeh ʾet-kol-hadddābār ʾăšer-yāṣāʾ mippînû lĕqaṭṭēr limlekhḗt haššāmayim — "We will do everything we vowed — making offerings to the queen of heaven (melekhḗt haššāmayim) and pouring out drink offerings to her"; the queen of heaven — the astral goddess Astarte/Ishtar/Inanna — whose cult was deeply embedded in popular Judean religion (cf. 7:18). The community's argument: when we worshipped her, we had plenty and were not in danger; when we stopped, disaster followed.
18: The theological logic of the community's argument: ûmiddê ḥādalnû lĕqaṭṭēr limlekhḗt haššāmayim... ḥāsarnû kōl wabbahhereb ûbārāʿāb tāmnû — "since we stopped making offerings to the queen of heaven, we have had nothing and have perished by sword and famine." The empirical argument: the correlation between stopping the cult and experiencing disaster. This is a genuine and serious theological objection — the community is offering a post-hoc explanation of their historical suffering that inverts Jeremiah's explanation.
19: The women clarify: we offered to the queen of heaven "without our husbands' knowledge (hamibbalʿadê ʾanšênû)?" — implying the husbands were participants. The women's defense of the cult and their claim to have made the offerings with household sanction shows this was communal, not clandestine. The MKT renders the women's speech as direct address to establish their public accountability for the argument.
20: wayyōʾmer Yirmĕyāhû ʾel-kol-hāʿām ʿal-hāʾănāšîm wĕʿal-hanāšîm — Jeremiah addresses the whole assembly — men and women both. The comprehensive address mirrors the comprehensive rejection (vv. 15-19). Jeremiah does not address one gender more than the other; the oracle applies to all who made or approved the offerings.
21: hălōʾ ʾet-haqqĕṭer ʾăšer qiṭṭartem bĕʿārê Yĕhûdâ — "The offerings you made in the cities of Judah and the streets of Jerusalem — you and your ancestors, your kings and your officials, and the people of the land — did not the LORD remember them?" YHWH's counter-argument: yes, YHWH remembered those offerings — and that is exactly why judgment fell. The community's argument (we sacrificed and prospered) is inverted: you sacrificed and YHWH was angry; the prosperity ended because of the offerings, not despite the lack of them.
22: wĕlōʾ-yūkal YHWH ʿôd śĕʾēt mippĕnê rōaʿ maʿălĕlêkem — "The LORD could no longer endure your evil deeds and the abominations you committed." The language of divine exhaustion: YHWH could bear it no longer (lōʾ-yūkal śĕʾēt). This is not a limitation of YHWH's power but of YHWH's patience — the divine long-suffering reaching its limit (cf. 2 Chr 36:16: "until the wrath of the LORD arose against his people until there was no remedy"). The MKT renders "could no longer endure" to convey the exhausted-patience meaning.
23: The rebuttal completed: yaʿan ʾăšer qiṭṭartem... ʿal-kēn qārʾat ʾetkem hārāʿâ hazzōʾt — "Because you burned offerings and sinned against the LORD... therefore this disaster has befallen you." The causal chain runs opposite to the community's version: not "we stopped offerings and disaster came" but "you burned offerings and disaster came."
24: Jeremiah's formal re-address: "Hear the word of the LORD, all you of Judah who are in the land of Egypt." The shift to the feminine plural (kol-Yĕhûdâ wĕkol-hanāšîm) in the following oracle (v.25) shows the women are specifically addressed for their leading role in the queen of heaven cult (vv. 17-19). The MKT preserves the shift in address as theologically significant.
25: kōh-ʾāmar YHWH ṣĕbāʾôt ʾĕlōhê Yiśrāʾēl lēʾmōr ʾattem wĕnĕšêkem — the ironic "permission" oracle: "You and your wives have stated with your mouths and fulfilled with your hands: 'We will surely keep our vows...' Go ahead, fulfill your vows!" YHWH releases them to fulfill their idolatrous vows — but the release is not a blessing; it is the withdrawal of covenant protection that makes the vow-keeping its own punishment. The MKT renders the ironic imperative: "Go ahead, then, fulfill your vows!"
26: nišbaʿtî bišmî haggādôl ʾāmar YHWH — "I have sworn by my great name, says the LORD"; the highest oath form available — YHWH swears by himself (cf. Gen 22:16; Isa 45:23; Heb 6:13). "My name will no longer be invoked by any person of Judah anywhere in Egypt" — the covenant-name relationship is severed. The community that rejected YHWH's word will lose access to YHWH's name. The MKT renders the divine self-oath solemnly: "sworn by my great name."
27: hinnĕnî šōqēd ʿălêhem lĕrāʿâ wĕlōʾ lĕṭôbâ — "I am watching over them for harm, not for good." The verb šōqēd (watching, alert) here reverses the positive watching of 1:12 (YHWH watches over his word to perform it for good). The same watchful alertness that maintained the covenant promise now maintains the covenant curse. Kōl ʾîš Yĕhûdâ... yittammû — all the men of Judah in Egypt will be consumed.
28: The remnant-within-a-remnant: a few (mĕtê mispar, men of number = a countable few) will return from Egypt to Judah. This exception maintains the possibility of a return and future; even in the judgment oracle, a remnant-principle survives. They will know whose word stands — YHWH's or the community's counter-claim. The MKT renders mĕtê mispar as "only a few" — countable rather than countless.
29: wĕzōʾt lākem hāʾôt nĕʾum-YHWH kî-pōqēd ʾănî ʿălêkem — "This will be your sign, declares the LORD, that I will punish you in this place." A sign oracle that points to a verifiable future event. The purpose of the sign: lĕmaʿan tēdĕʿû kî qôm yāqûmû dĕbāray ʿălêkem lĕrāʿâ — "so you may know that my words of judgment against you will surely stand." The qôm yāqûm (surely stand) mirrors Jeremiah's credibility test for true prophecy in 28:9.
30: hinnĕnî nōtēn ʾet-Parʿōh Ḥopraʿ melek Miṣrayim bĕyad ʾōyĕbāyw — "I am going to hand over Pharaoh Hophra king of Egypt to his enemies and to those who seek his life, just as I handed over Zedekiah king of Judah." The explicit parallel: Hophra/Egypt = Zedekiah/Judah. The fate of the Egyptian king will mirror the fate of the Judean king — historical verification of the oracle. Hophra (Apries) was indeed killed in a revolt c. 570 BC, within Jeremiah's likely lifetime. The MKT preserves "just as I handed over Zedekiah" as the precise comparative.
